title: Small Deviations of Sums of Independent Random Variables
overall_summary: The paper improves Feige-type small-deviation lower bounds for sums of independent nonnegative random variables with individual means at most 1, proving Pr[Î£_i X_i < E[Î£_i X_i] + 1] â‰¥ 7/50, and in fact a slightly better explicit constant. The core method is a fourth-moment duality argument: construct explicit quartic polynomials Q_{â„“,r} majorizing one-sided indicators, then optimize their expectations using first through fourth moment estimates and, crucially, the sign of the third central moment. The paper proves several reusable one-sided bounds for 4-wise independent bounded mean-zero sums, including refinements when the third moment is nonnegative and when the summands are two-point with nonpositive third moment. It also gives exact linear-programming dual calculations for tail probabilities of 2-wise and 3-wise independent Bernoulli sums, yielding counterexamples to small-deviation bounds under only pairwise or 3-wise independence. For the original weighted Bernoulli problem, the most relevant ingredients are the quartic indicator majorants, the two-point moment identities, the split according to third-moment sign, and the Bernoulli linear-programming viewpoint.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.2 and equation (11): quartic one-sided majorant Q_{â„“,r}
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let â„“>0 and r>0. Define Q_{â„“,r}(x)=Î£_{i=0}^4 q_i x^i, where q_0=1, q_1=2r^2(2â„“+r)/(â„“(â„“+r)^3), q_2=r(-8â„“^2-â„“r+r^2)/(â„“^2(â„“+r)^3), q_3=(4â„“^2-4â„“r-2r^2)/(â„“^2(â„“+r)^3), and q_4=(3â„“+r)/(â„“^2(â„“+r)^3). Then Q_{â„“,r}(x) â‰¥ 1{xâ‰¥0} for every real x. If X is a real random variable with E[X]=0 and finite fourth moment, then for every Î´>0, E[Q_{â„“,r}(X-Î´)] = Î£_{i=0}^4 (-Î´)^i q_i + (q_2-3Î´q_3+6Î´^2q_4)E[X^2] + (q_3-4Î´q_4)E[X^3] + q_4E[X^4]. Also q_4>0 always, and q_3<0 whenever â„“â‰¤(1+sqrt(3))r/2; in particular q_3<0 when â„“â‰¤r. In the special case â„“=r, denoting the polynomial by Q_r, one has Q_r(x)=1+3x/(4r)-x^2/r^2-x^3/(4r^3)+x^4/(2r^4).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by explicit factorization. The polynomial can be written as Q_{â„“,r}(x) = ((â„“+x)^2/((â„“^2)(â„“+r)^3))*((â„“+r)^3 - 2(â„“^2+3â„“r+r^2)x + (3â„“+r)x^2). The quadratic factor has minimum at x=(â„“^2+3â„“r+r^2)/(3â„“+r), and the corresponding numerator is ((3â„“+r)(â„“+r)^3-(â„“^2+3â„“r+r^2)^2)/(3â„“+r) = (2â„“^4+4â„“^3r+â„“^2r^2)/(3â„“+r), which is nonnegative. Hence Q_{â„“,r}(x)â‰¥0 for all x. A second factorization gives Q_{â„“,r}(x)-1 = (x(4â„“^2+2â„“r+(3â„“+r)x)(x-r)^2)/(â„“^2(â„“+r)^3), which is nonnegative for xâ‰¥0; combining this with Q_{â„“,r}â‰¥0 proves Q_{â„“,r}â‰¥1{xâ‰¥0}. The expectation identity follows by expanding Q_{â„“,r}(X-Î´), using E[X]=0, and collecting coefficients of E[X^2], E[X^3], and E[X^4]. The sign facts follow immediately from the displayed formulas for q_3 and q_4.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.2 / Theorem 2.1: moment bound with nonnegative third moment
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a real random variable with E[X]=0, E[X^2]=Ïƒ^2, and E[X^3]â‰¥0. If E[X^4]â‰¤cÏƒ^4, then Pr[Xâ‰¥0]â‰¤1-1/(2c).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the quartic majorant Q_r with r=sqrt(c)Ïƒ, namely Q(x)=1+3x/(4sqrt(c)Ïƒ)-x^2/(cÏƒ^2)-x^3/(4c^{3/2}Ïƒ^3)+x^4/(2c^2Ïƒ^4). By Lemma 2.2, 1{xâ‰¥0}â‰¤Q(x), so Pr[Xâ‰¥0]â‰¤E[Q(X)]. Since E[X]=0, the linear term vanishes. The quadratic contribution is -E[X^2]/(cÏƒ^2)=-1/c. The cubic coefficient is negative and E[X^3]â‰¥0, so the cubic term is nonpositive and can be dropped for an upper bound. The fourth term is at most E[X^4]/(2c^2Ïƒ^4)â‰¤1/(2c). Thus E[Q(X)]â‰¤1-1/c+1/(2c)=1-1/(2c). The paper notes tightness: for any a>0 and p<1/2, the law with mass p at -a, mass 1-2p at 0, and mass p at a has E[X^3]=0 and attains equality for the corresponding value of c.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 2.1 moment estimates for bounded centered sums
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X_1,...,X_n be independent mean-zero random variables, let X=Î£_i X_i, and set Ïƒ^2=E[X^2]. If |X_i|â‰¤1 for every i, then |E[X^3]|â‰¤Ïƒ^2 and E[X^4]â‰¤3Ïƒ^4+Ïƒ^2. The paper observes that the same moment computations only require 4-wise independence.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Because the summands are centered and independent, all mixed third-moment terms vanish, so E[X^3]=Î£_i E[X_i^3]. Since |X_i|â‰¤1, |E[X_i^3]|â‰¤E[|X_i|^3]â‰¤E[X_i^2], and summing gives |E[X^3]|â‰¤Î£_i E[X_i^2]=E[X^2]=Ïƒ^2. For the fourth moment, independence and centering give E[X^4]=Î£_i E[X_i^4]+6Î£_{i<j}E[X_i^2]E[X_j^2]. Rewriting, E[X^4]=3(Î£_i E[X_i^2])^2+Î£_i(E[X_i^4]-3E[X_i^2]^2). Since |X_i|â‰¤1 implies E[X_i^4]â‰¤E[X_i^2], the last display is at most 3Ïƒ^4+Î£_i E[X_i^2]=3Ïƒ^4+Ïƒ^2. Only joint moments involving at most four variables are used, hence 4-wise independence suffices.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.3 / Theorem 2.3: bounded 4-wise independent small deviation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X_1,...,X_n be a 4-wise independent collection of random variables such that E[X_i]=0 and |X_i|â‰¤1 for every i. Let X=Î£_i X_i. Then Pr[X<Î´]â‰¥1/6 for every Î´â‰¥1/3; equivalently, Pr[Xâ‰¥1/3]â‰¤5/6.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let Ïƒ^2=E[X^2]. The bounded-sum moment estimates give E[X^3]â‰¥-Ïƒ^2 and E[X^4]â‰¤3Ïƒ^4+Ïƒ^2. Apply the majorant Q_{â„“,r} to X-Î´ with Î´=1/3. For choices with â„“â‰¤r, q_3<0 and q_4>0, so q_3-4Î´q_4<0; replacing E[X^3] by its lower bound -Ïƒ^2 and E[X^4] by its upper bound 3Ïƒ^4+Ïƒ^2 yields an explicit upper bound for E[Q_{â„“,r}(X-1/3)]. If â„“=r=sqrt(3)Ïƒ, equation (15) gives E[Q_{sqrt(3)Ïƒ}(X-1/3)]â‰¤5/6 + (1/(36Ïƒ^4))*(2/81 + (sqrt(3)/27)Ïƒ + (14/3)Ïƒ^2 - sqrt(3)Ïƒ^3), which is â‰¤5/6 for Ïƒâ‰¥3. For intermediate Ïƒ, the paper sets r=aÏƒ and â„“=r, obtaining B_a(Ïƒ)=(3-2a^2+2a^4)/(2a^4)+(2-a^2)/(4a^3)Ïƒ^{-1}+(27-2a^2)/(18a^4)Ïƒ^{-2}+1/(108a^3)Ïƒ^{-3}+1/(162a^4)Ïƒ^{-4}. When 27-2a^2â‰¥0 this is convex as a polynomial in Ïƒ^{-1}; checking endpoints proves B_2â‰¤5/6 on Ïƒâˆˆ[3/2,3], B_{9/4}â‰¤5/6 on Ïƒâˆˆ[1,3/2], and B_{5/2}â‰¤5/6 on Ïƒâˆˆ[1/2,1]. Finally, for Ïƒ<1/2 the fixed choice r=3/2 gives E[Q_{3/2}(X-1/3)]â‰¤10339/13122+(8/27)Ïƒ^4â‰¤5/6. Since Q majorizes 1{xâ‰¥0}, E[Q(X-1/3)] bounds Pr[Xâ‰¥1/3], completing the proof.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.1: positive-third-moment refinement
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X_1,...,X_n be a 4-wise independent collection of random variables such that E[X_i]=0 and |X_i|â‰¤1 for every i. Let X=Î£_i X_i. If E[X^3]â‰¥0, then Pr[Xâ‰¥4/25]â‰¤5/6, equivalently Pr[X<4/25]â‰¥1/6.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Again set Ïƒ^2=E[X^2]. The fourth moment estimate gives E[X^4]â‰¤3Ïƒ^4+Ïƒ^2. In the expectation identity for Q_{â„“,r}(X-Î´), take Î´=4/25 and choose â„“â‰¤r, so q_3-4Î´q_4<0. Since E[X^3]â‰¥0, the cubic contribution is nonpositive and can be discarded rather than bounded by -Ïƒ^2; this is the key improvement over Theorem 2.3. With â„“=r=sqrt(3)Ïƒ, the paper obtains E[Q_{sqrt(3)Ïƒ}(X-Î´)]â‰¤5/6 + (2Î´^4+sqrt(3)Î´^3Ïƒ+2Ïƒ^2-6sqrt(3)Î´Ïƒ^3)/(36Ïƒ^4), and for Î´=4/25 this is â‰¤5/6 for Ïƒ>5/4. For Ïƒâˆˆ[0.68,1.25], it takes â„“=2Ïƒ and r=5Ïƒ/2 and obtains the upper bound 835/972 - (226Î´/729)Ïƒ^{-1} + ((68-207Î´^2)/2916)Ïƒ^{-2} + (11Î´^3/243)Ïƒ^{-3} + (17Î´^4/729)Ïƒ^{-4}, which is convex in Ïƒ^{-1} for Î´=4/25 and is checked at the two endpoints. For Ïƒâˆˆ[0.5,0.68], it takes â„“=15Ïƒ/7 and r=3Ïƒ and obtains 256957/291600 - (10633Î´/32400)Ïƒ^{-1} + ((26411-128625Î´^2)/1749600)Ïƒ^{-2} + (7889Î´^3/194400)Ïƒ^{-3} + (26411Î´^4/1749600)Ïƒ^{-4}, again convex in Ïƒ^{-1} and endpoint-checked. For Ïƒâˆˆ[0,0.5], it uses â„“=1 and r=2, giving E[Q_{1,2}(X-4/25)]â‰¤62573/78125 - (59/3375)Ïƒ^2 + (5/9)Ïƒ^4â‰¤5/6. These cases cover all Ïƒ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.2: two-point negative-third-moment refinement
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X_1,...,X_n be a 4-wise independent collection of mean-zero random variables. For each i, suppose X_i has support {-a_i,b_i}, where a_i>0 and b_i>0, and suppose a_iâ‰¤1 for every i. Assume b_1=max_i b_i and a_1â‰¥1/16. Let X=Î£_i X_i. If E[X^3]â‰¤0, then Pr[Xâ‰¥1]â‰¤5/6, equivalently Pr[X<1]â‰¥1/6.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a mean-zero two-point variable with support {-a_i,b_i}, the probabilities are Pr[X_i=-a_i]=b_i/(a_i+b_i) and Pr[X_i=b_i]=a_i/(a_i+b_i). Hence E[X_i^2]=a_i b_i and E[X_i^3]=a_i b_i(b_i-a_i). Set Ïƒ^2=E[X^2]=Î£_i a_i b_i. Since a_iâ‰¤1, E[X^3]=Î£_i a_i b_i(b_i-a_i)â‰¥-Î£_i a_i^2b_iâ‰¥-Ïƒ^2. For the fourth moment, the standard fourth-moment expansion gives E[X^4]â‰¤3Ïƒ^4+Î£_i E[X_i^4], and the two-point formula is bounded by E[X_i^4]â‰¤a_i b_i(a_i^2+b_i^2). Thus E[X^4]â‰¤3Ïƒ^4+Î£_i a_i^3b_i+Î£_i a_i b_i^3â‰¤3Ïƒ^4+Ïƒ^2+Î£_i a_i b_i^3. The assumption E[X^3]â‰¤0 implies Î£_i a_i b_i^2â‰¤Î£_i a_i^2b_iâ‰¤Ïƒ^2. Since b_1 is maximal and a_1â‰¥1/16, Î£_i a_i b_i^3â‰¤b_1Î£_i a_i b_i^2â‰¤b_1Ïƒ^2â‰¤4sqrt(Î£_i a_i b_i^2)Ïƒ^2â‰¤4Ïƒ^3. Therefore E[X^4]â‰¤3Ïƒ^4+4Ïƒ^3+Ïƒ^2. Applying the expectation identity to Q_{â„“,r}(X-1) with â„“=r choices and using E[X^3]â‰¥-Ïƒ^2 gives E[Q_{â„“,r}(X-1)]â‰¤Î£_i(-1)^i q_i+(q_2-3q_3+6q_4)Ïƒ^2+(q_3-4q_4)(-Ïƒ^2)+q_4(3Ïƒ^4+4Ïƒ^3+Ïƒ^2). With r=â„“=sqrt(3)Ïƒ this becomes at most 5/6 + (2+sqrt(3)Ïƒ+10Ïƒ^2+(8-5sqrt(3))Ïƒ^3)/(36Ïƒ^4), which is â‰¤5/6 for Ïƒâ‰¥16. For Ïƒâˆˆ[5/2,16], the paper chooses r=â„“=19Ïƒ/10 and obtains (218442-24885Ïƒ^{-1}+37800Ïƒ^{-2}+9500Ïƒ^{-3}+10000Ïƒ^{-4})/260642, a convex polynomial in Ïƒ^{-1} that is endpoint-checked to be â‰¤5/6. For Ïƒâˆˆ[0,5/2], it chooses r=â„“=5 and obtains (1016-29Ïƒ^2+4Ïƒ^3+3Ïƒ^4)/1250â‰¤5/6 by elementary calculus. Thus Pr[Xâ‰¥1]â‰¤E[Q(X-1)]â‰¤5/6.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.1 / Theorem 3.3: improved Feige-type small-deviation bound
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X_1,...,X_n be nonnegative independent random variables with means Î¼_i=E[X_i] satisfying Î¼_iâ‰¤1 for every i. Then Pr[Î£_i X_i < Î£_i Î¼_i + 1] â‰¥ Î², where Î²=(46/279)e^{-4/25}; in particular Î²>7/50.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof begins with Feige's reductions, cited from [2]. By a linear-programming argument one may assume each X_i is nonconstant and has support of size two; subtracting the lower support point makes each support {0,c_i} without changing the target probability, possibly reducing Î¼_i. A merge operation combines the two variables of smallest means when one has mean below a threshold t and the other has mean at most 1-t, then again reduces to a two-point support at 0; in the proof t is used as 1/16, so at termination there is at most one mean below 1/16 and all other means exceed 15/16. Write s_i=c_i-Î¼_i and sort so s_1â‰¥...â‰¥s_n. Fix Ï„=25/4 and define k=max(0,max{j: s_jâ‰¥Ï„(Î¼_1+...+Î¼_j)}); let m=Î£_{iâ‰¤k}Î¼_i. For i>k, the stopping rule gives s_iâ‰¤Ï„(m+Î¼_{k+1})â‰¤Ï„(m+1). For iâ‰¤k, s_iâ‰¥Ï„m, so if k>0 then Pr[X_1=...=X_k=0]=Î _{iâ‰¤k}(1-Î¼_i/(s_i+Î¼_i))â‰¥Î _{iâ‰¤k}exp(-Î¼_i/(Ï„m))=e^{-1/Ï„}=e^{-4/25}. Conditioning on these first k variables being 0 reduces the problem to centered variables Y_j=X_{k+j}-Î¼_{k+j}, j=1,...,n-k, with supports {-a_j,b_j}, where a_jâ‰¤1 and b_jâ‰¤(25/4)(m+1). If E[(Î£_j Y_j)^3]â‰¥0, scale Y_j by 4/(25(m+1)) and apply Lemma 3.1 to get Pr[Î£_j Y_j<m+1]â‰¥1/6, hence the original probability is at least e^{-4/25}/6>Î². If E[(Î£_j Y_j)^3]<0 and the largest-positive-support variable satisfies a_1â‰¥1/16, Lemma 3.2 gives Pr[Î£_j Y_j<1]â‰¥1/6 and hence Pr[Î£_j Y_j<m+1]â‰¥1/6. If instead a_1<1/16, the merge stopping condition implies all other means are >15/16. If b_1<3(m+1), scaling by 1/(3(m+1)) and applying Theorem 2.3 again gives probability at least 1/6. Thus one may assume b_1â‰¥3(m+1). Then Pr[Y_1=b_1]=a_1/(a_1+b_1)â‰¤1/(48(m+1)); condition on Y_1 not taking this positive value, which only helps the upper-tail event. Removing Y_1 leaves the third moment still negative because E[Y_1^3]=a_1b_1(b_1-a_1)>0, and the remaining variables have a_j>1/16, so Lemma 3.2 applies. If k=0, this gives at least (1-1/48)/6>Î². If kâ‰¥1, then mâ‰¥15/16, so the total lower bound is at least e^{-4/25}(1-1/(48(m+1)))(1/6)â‰¥e^{-4/25}(92/93)(1/6)=Î².

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 4, k=2 Bernoulli linear program and Theorem 1.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A(n,2,p) be the set of collections of n pairwise independent Bernoulli random variables with common marginal probability p, let S be their sum, and let m be an integer threshold. In the regime mâ‰¤np+1-p, the maximum of Pr[Sâ‰¥m] over A(n,2,p) equals p(n+m-np-1+p)/m. An optimal exchangeable distribution for S is supported on {0,m,n} with probabilities p_0=(1-p)(m-np+p)/m, p_m=p(1-p)n(n-1)/(m(n-m)), and p_n=p(np-m+1-p)/(n-m). Equivalently, when m=(n+d)p is an integer and pâ‰¤1/(d+1), this maximum is p(n+m-np-1+p)/m; it is maximized at p=1/(d+1), where p_0=d/(n+d), p_m=n/(n+d), and p_n=0. Consequently, Theorem 1.4 states: for Î´>0 such that (n+Î´)/(Î´+1) is an integer, there exist nonnegative pairwise independent random variables X_1,...,X_n, each with mean 1, such that Pr[Î£_i X_i<n+Î´]=Î´/(n+Î´).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper formulates a linear program for k-wise independent Bernoulli variables. Because the event {Sâ‰¥m} is symmetric, one may symmetrize and work with probabilities p_r=Pr[S=r], r=0,...,n. Pairwise independence with common marginal p is encoded by matching the first two moments of S to those of Bin(n,p): Î£_r r^i p_r=E[Bin(n,p)^i] for i=0,1,2. The dual minimizes E[Q(B)] for B~Bin(n,p) over degree-2 polynomials Q with Q(j)â‰¥0 for j<m and Q(j)â‰¥1 for jâ‰¥m. The polynomial f(x)=((m+n)x-x^2)/(mn) is feasible: f(0)=0, f(m)=f(n)=1, it is nonnegative below m, and by concavity it is at least 1 on the interval from m to n. Its binomial expectation equals p(n+m-np-1+p)/m. Complementary slackness with support at the points where f is 0 or 1 gives the displayed primal probabilities. For Theorem 1.4, set d=Î´ and p=1/(Î´+1), choose pairwise independent Bernoulli variables B_i with the optimal law, and scale X_i=B_i/p=(Î´+1)B_i. Then E[X_i]=1 and Î£_i X_i<n+Î´ is equivalent to Î£_i B_i<(n+Î´)p=m, which has probability p_0=Î´/(n+Î´).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 4, k=3 Bernoulli linear program and Theorem 1.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A(n,3,p) be the set of collections of n 3-wise independent Bernoulli random variables with common marginal probability p, let S be their sum, and let m be an integer threshold. In the regime mâ‰¤np+1-2p, the maximum of Pr[Sâ‰¥m] over A(n,3,p) equals p((n-2)(1-p)^2+m(2-p))/m. The corresponding optimal distribution is supported on {0,m,n-1,n}. When m=(n+d)p is an integer and pâ‰¤1/(d+2), the maximum is maximized at p=1/(d+2), and the optimal probabilities simplify to p_0=(d+1)^2/((d+2)(n+d)), p_m=(d+1)n(n-1)/((n+d)(n+nd-d)), p_{n-1}=0, and p_n=1/((d+2)(n+nd-d)). Consequently, Theorem 1.5 states: for Î´>0 such that (n+Î´)/(Î´+2) is an integer, there exist nonnegative 3-wise independent random variables X_1,...,X_n, each with mean 1, such that Pr[Î£_i X_i<n+Î´]=((Î´+1)^2)/((Î´+2)(n+Î´)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: As in the pairwise case, symmetrization reduces the primal problem to variables p_r=Pr[S=r] with moment constraints matching Bin(n,p) up to degree 3. The dual minimizes E[Q(B)] over cubic polynomials Q satisfying Q(j)â‰¥0 below the threshold m and Q(j)â‰¥1 at and above m. The paper gives the feasible cubic g(x)=((n^2+2mn-n-m)x-(2n+m-1)x^2+x^3)/(n(n-1)m), which satisfies g(0)=0 and g(m)=g(n-1)=g(n)=1; checking feasibility and evaluating E[g(B)] yields p((n-2)(1-p)^2+m(2-p))/m. Complementary slackness then identifies the optimal support {0,m,n-1,n} and the displayed probabilities in the special case p=1/(d+2). For Theorem 1.5, take d=Î´, p=1/(Î´+2), construct Bernoulli variables B_i with this optimal 3-wise independent law, and scale X_i=B_i/p=(Î´+2)B_i. Then E[X_i]=1 and the event Î£_i X_i<n+Î´ is the same as Î£_i B_i<m, whose probability is p_0=((Î´+1)^2)/((Î´+2)(n+Î´)).

proof_techniques.name: Moment-problem duality with polynomial majorants
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly uses the dual of a truncated moment problem: to upper-bound Pr[X in S] from finitely many moments, find a low-degree polynomial Q that majorizes 1_S pointwise and then bound E[Q(X)] using the available moments. For one-sided events S={xâ‰¥Î´}, it shifts the variable and uses the quartic Q_{â„“,r}, designed to have a double root at -â„“, value 1 at 0, and Q-1 with a double root at r. The factorization of Q and Q-1 gives pointwise feasibility, while the free parameters â„“ and r allow optimization in different variance regimes.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a weighted Bernoulli sum, centering gives a sum of independent two-point mean-zero variables; polynomial majorants can bound either Pr[Î£ w_i(v_i-p)â‰¥0] or the complementary side after changing signs. The parameters â„“,r could be optimized using the explicit Bernoulli moments as functions of p and the weights.

proof_techniques.name: Exploit the sign of the third central moment
proof_techniques.description: Unlike earlier fourth-moment-only arguments, the paper keeps the cubic term in E[Q(X)]. For the quartics used here, q_3 is negative when â„“â‰¤r, and after shifting by Î´ the coefficient q_3-4Î´q_4 remains negative. If E[X^3]â‰¥0, this term improves the upper bound and can be discarded as nonpositive; if E[X^3]<0, it is controlled by lower bounds such as E[X^3]â‰¥-Ïƒ^2 or by two-point structure.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For centered Bernoulli-type summands, the third central moment has a simple sign depending on the Bernoulli bias and the chosen orientation. Splitting the original problem into pâ‰¤1/2 and pâ‰¥1/2, or applying the argument to the negative centered sum, is a natural way to use this idea.

proof_techniques.name: Two-point moment identities and skewness control
proof_techniques.description: For a mean-zero variable supported on {-a,b}, the paper uses Pr[-a]=b/(a+b), Pr[b]=a/(a+b), E[X^2]=ab, and E[X^3]=ab(b-a). Under a nonpositive third moment assumption for a sum of such variables, it derives Î£_i a_i b_i^2â‰¤Î£_i a_i^2 b_iâ‰¤Ïƒ^2. Combined with a lower bound on the a_i corresponding to the largest b_i, this controls the otherwise unbounded fourth-moment contribution Î£_i a_i b_i^3 by 4Ïƒ^3.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Each weighted Bernoulli summand after centering is exactly two-point. These identities allow one to express all low moments in terms of p and the weights and may help isolate or control heavy weights that dominate the tail probability.

proof_techniques.name: Variance-regime polynomial tuning
proof_techniques.description: The proofs do not use a single quartic for all variance values. They choose â„“ and r as multiples of Ïƒ for large and medium Ïƒ, and fixed â„“,r for small Ïƒ. After substitution, the expectation bound is often a polynomial in Ïƒ^{-1}; when the coefficient of Ïƒ^{-2} is nonnegative, convexity in Ïƒ^{-1} reduces verification on an interval to checking endpoints.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If optimizing over weights reduces to controlling the variance Ïƒ^2=p(1-p)Î£_i w_i^2 or similar scale parameters, a case split between diffuse weights and heavy weights could make polynomial inequalities tractable.

proof_techniques.name: Conditioning away large-surplus variables
proof_techniques.description: In the main theorem, after reducing variables to two-point supports {0,c_i}, the proof sorts by surplus s_i=c_i-Î¼_i and chooses an initial block whose surpluses are large relative to their total mean. The event that all variables in this block are zero has probability at least e^{-1/Ï„}; conditioning on it leaves a remainder whose positive supports are bounded by Ï„ times the allowed deviation. This allows bounded-sum lemmas to be applied to the remainder.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For weighted Bernoulli sums, large weights may be treated separately by conditioning on whether the corresponding Bernoulli variables are zero or one. This can reduce the problem to a bounded or more homogeneous remainder while paying an explicit probability factor.

proof_techniques.name: Merge/reduction to two-point variables
proof_techniques.description: The main theorem invokes Feige's linear-programming reduction showing that, for the small-deviation lower bound considered there, one may assume variables are nonconstant with two-point support, then shift the lower support to zero. A merge operation combines two small-mean variables without increasing the bad probability, producing a terminal configuration in which at most one mean is below a chosen threshold and all others are large.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem already has two-point summands, so the support reduction is not needed, but the merge philosophy suggests searching for extremal weight configurations with few distinct scales or with one exceptional small component.

proof_techniques.name: Linear programming duality for k-wise independent Bernoulli sums
proof_techniques.description: Section 4 formulates the maximum tail probability of a k-wise independent Bernoulli sum as a finite linear program in the probabilities p_r=Pr[S=r]. Moment constraints up to degree k match those of Bin(n,p), and the dual searches for degree-k polynomials that are nonnegative below the threshold and at least 1 above it. For k=2 and k=3, explicit optimal polynomials identify the optimal primal support by complementary slackness.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Although the original variables are fully independent and weighted, the same polynomial-dual mindset may help identify extremal distributions or certify inequalities for symmetric or equal-weight subcases. The k-wise examples also warn that low-moment information alone may be insufficient for sharp Bernoulli tail statements.

other_useful_info: The introduction recalls Feige's conjectured optimal constant 1/e for the bound Pr[Î£_i X_i < EÎ£_i X_i + 1] under independent nonnegative variables with means at most 1; the paper gives the standard tightness example where each X_i has mean 1 and support {0,n+Î´}. For smaller deviations, it records the example with one variable supported on {0,1+Î´} and the rest constant, giving probability Î´/(1+Î´). The paper states that the 1/6 constant in Theorem 1.3 is best possible for the 4-wise independent bounded setting, and that the deviation threshold there cannot be lowered below 1/5; these tightness facts are attributed to Garnett's thesis [3]. It also remarks that, because Theorem 1.3 and the Section 3 lemmas are tight in that fourth-moment framework, one cannot obtain a constant larger than 1/6 in the main theorem using only first four moments; possible improvements would require either lowering the Î´=4/25 in Lemma 3.1 or using higher even moments. The Bernoulli linear-programming setup in Section 4 is connected to Peled, Yadin, and Yehudayoff [8], and the two-point reduction/merge transformations are attributed to Feige [2].
why_relevant: Improves Feige-type small-deviation bounds using moment methods and discusses Samuels' conjecture and two-point/Bernoulli subclasses. The Bernoulli-specialized arguments are likely useful for optimizing over the weights.
